title: Program, source code, and algorithm

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific pilot lesson awaiting accuracy, pedagogy, and accessibility review by a human editor. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Software Development Fundamentals knowledge area, introductory programming concepts
sources.claim: Beginners should distinguish problem-solving procedures, source representations, and executing software artifacts.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An algorithm is a finite method for solving a class of problems; source code expresses such a method in a programming language; a program is the complete executable software artifact and its required context.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A class has the scores 62, 91, and 74, and the required output is the largest score. Your goal is to classify concrete artifacts as an algorithm, source code, a running program, an input, or an output and justify the classification. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: This skill has no declared earlier lesson. The course assumes: Basic reading and arithmetic; Ability to use a keyboard and web browser. You can continue when those ideas feel familiar.

sections.heading: Three related objects
sections.body: The algorithm describes what steps solve the problem without depending on one particular spelling. Source code is text written under a language's syntax and meaning rules. A program is what runs: translated or interpreted instructions together with the data and environment they require.

sections.heading: Why the distinction matters
sections.body: One algorithm can be expressed in several languages, and one source file can be only one part of a larger program. When a result is wrong, naming the layer helps: the algorithm may be flawed, the source may mistranslate it, or the running program may receive unexpected input or environment state.

includes: Classifying algorithms, source code, programs, inputs, and outputs
Relating one algorithm to multiple possible source-code expressions
excludes: Formal mathematical proofs of algorithm correctness

outcomes: Classify concrete artifacts as an algorithm, source code, a running program, an input, or an output and justify the classification.

examples.title: Finding the largest score
examples.situation: A class has the scores 62, 91, and 74, and the required output is the largest score.
walkthrough: Step 1 — The algorithm keeps a current largest value and compares each remaining score with it.
Step 2 — Python or Java source code can express those same comparisons using different syntax.
Step 3 — When that source is executed with the three scores as input, the program produces 91 as output.
examples.result: The comparison method is the algorithm, the language text is source code, and the executing artifact that prints 91 is the program.

examples.title: Choosing the shorter route
examples.situation: A navigation feature receives two route distances and must display the shorter one.
walkthrough: Step 1 — The reusable algorithm compares two distances and selects the smaller value.
Step 2 — The source contains a concrete conditional expression plus code for reading and displaying values.
Step 3 — The running program combines that source's behavior with a device, an operating environment, and the actual distance input.
examples.result: The same comparison algorithm can appear in a phone app or command-line program without becoming the source code itself.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every source file is automatically a complete program that can run by itself.
misconceptions.correction: A source file may be incomplete, may depend on other files or libraries, and may need translation before any program can execute.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: A file contains one helper function but no entry point or caller; is it source code, a complete runnable program, both, or neither, and why?

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Changing from Python syntax to Java syntax necessarily creates a different algorithm.
misconceptions.correction: Different source expressions can implement the same ordered method, although implementation details can affect behavior or performance.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Two snippets both scan every score and retain the greatest value; what evidence would show they implement the same algorithm?

analogy.example: A recipe's method resembles an algorithm, the written recipe resembles source code, and a cook carrying it out with real ingredients resembles a running program.
usefulFor: Separating a reusable procedure from one written representation and one execution
limitations: Computers follow formal language rules more literally than cooks follow recipes
A deployed program can contain many source files, libraries, generated assets, and environmental dependencies

artifact: ALGORITHM scan values and retain the greatest

steps.explanation: The problem first declares actual input and the observable result that is required.
The ordered solution method is described without committing to a programming-language spelling.
Source code expresses the method using one language's syntax, types, and control structures.
The program runs the expressed method in a real environment and produces the observable output 91.

trace.textAlternative: Starting artifact: INPUT scores = [62, 91, 74] Step 1, Problem. State: input: [62, 91, 74]; output: unknown. The problem first declares actual input and the observable result that is required. Step 2, Algorithm. State: method: scan and retain greatest; language: none. The ordered solution method is described without committing to a programming-language spelling. Step 3, Source code. State: method: scan and retain greatest; language: chosen. Source code expresses the method using one language's syntax, types, and control structures. Step 4, Program execution. State: input: [62, 91, 74]; output: 91. The program runs the expressed method in a real environment and produces the observable output 91.

faded.prompt: Quick check for “Finding the largest score”: state its starting evidence, apply the Program, source code, and algorithm rule, and give the observable result in three short lines.
scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.
expectedEvidence: Named example: Finding the largest score.
Starting evidence: A class has the scores 62, 91, and 74, and the required output is the largest score.

Core rule: An algorithm is a finite method for solving a class of problems; source code expresses such a method in a programming language; a program is the complete executable software artifact and its required context.
Observable result: The comparison method is the algorithm, the language text is source code, and the executing artifact that prints 91 is the program.
Boundary check: A source file may be incomplete, may depend on other files or libraries, and may need translation before any program can execute.

nearTransfer.prompt: Classify a pseudocode procedure, a Python file, a running Python process, keyboard text, and displayed text in a word-count scenario.
scaffold: Ask whether each artifact is a method, representation, execution, incoming data, or outgoing data
expectedEvidence: Each artifact receives one justified classification
The learner distinguishes the file from its executing process

farTransfer.prompt: A spreadsheet formula and a web service both calculate loan interest. Explain what could be the shared algorithm and what counts as source and program in each environment.
scaffold: Ignore surface appearance and identify the ordered calculation
expectedEvidence: The shared calculation is stated independently of either tool
The explanation recognizes two different implementations and execution environments

remediation.explanation: Classify by role, not filename: readable language text is source, while a runnable program needs a valid entry path and every required dependency.
remediation.retryPrompt: Classify a library file containing reusable functions and explain what is still needed before its behavior can execute.

remediation.explanation: Compare the sequence of decisions and state changes first; syntax is the expression layer, while those abstract operations identify the algorithm.
remediation.retryPrompt: Describe one maximum-finding algorithm, then name two visibly different source-code forms that could implement it.

recap.summary: You should now be able to classify concrete artifacts as an algorithm, source code, a running program, an input, or an output and justify the classification. Keep this boundary in view: Compiler internals and machine-code formats
retrievalPrompts: Without using the recipe analogy, distinguish an algorithm from source code.
Why might one source file fail to be a complete runnable program?
Give one input and one output for a program that implements a maximum-finding algorithm.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Hardware and software roles”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
